title: Why we stopped making land - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: US urban land reclamation ceased abruptly in the 1970s due to environmental regulation, even as land values rose thirtyfold since 1950 and high-tide flooding grew four-to-eightfold, leaving reclamation more economically justified than ever yet essentially abandoned.

themes.theme: The abrupt halt of land reclamation

insights.claim: Around eight percent of the land in America's major coastal cities was underwater in the 1890s and has since been reclaimed, including sites now holding Newark, Logan, and SFO airports and San Francisco's Financial District.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Critical existing infrastructure sits on reclaimed land, underscoring how central reclamation once was to urban economic capacity.
insights.quote: around eight percent of the land in America's major coastal cities was underwater in the 1890s and has since been reclaimed

insights.claim: No major US urban land reclamation project has occurred since Battery Park City was completed in 1976, despite land values in some cities being thirty times their 1950 level and high-tide flooding being four to eight times more frequent.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A large latent supply-side lever on coastal land and flood defense sits unused; regulatory reform could unlock significant real-estate value creation.
insights.quote: Since the completion of Battery Park City in 1976, there has not been a single major urban land reclamation project in the United States
insights.locator: Today, reclamation should be more common

insights.claim: Reclamation stopped abruptly in the 1970s when a wave of environmental regulations made reshaping the landscape enormously expensive, and it halted simultaneously in every other country that passed similar laws.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Identifies environmental regulation as the specific binding constraint, a policy variable whose change would predictably revive reclamation globally.
insights.quote: Reclamation stopped abruptly in the 1970s when a wave of environmental regulations made it enormously expensive to reshape the landscape.
